description: A workbench that withstands years of ordinary use is 'keruko'. It describes construction that resists damage. 'kema' (strong) instead refers to force exerted or withstood. A sturdy thing may still fail when used far beyond what it was built to handle.
articulatory_notes: Make initial 'k' by pressing the back of the tongue to the soft palate, then release into true-mid front 'e'. The stressed second syllable starts with a tap or short trill before rounded high-back 'u'. Close at the velum once more for final 'k' and release into rounded mid-back 'o'.
sound_symbolism: The tongue makes the same complete velar closure twice, at the beginning and near the end of 'keruko'. Between them, the brief tap keeps the word from sounding heavy throughout. That return to full contact provides a small echo of sturdy construction.
usage_notes: 'keruko lopha' is a sturdy vessel. Expected use sets the comparison; a sturdy basket and a sturdy bridge face different demands.

semantic_domains.creation: construction able to withstand its expected use
semantic_domains.physical: resistance to damage during handling or use
